response:
To find the equilibrium population size we set the rate of change to zero meaning dPdt equals zero. The equation becomes zero equals r times P times one minus P over K minus h(t). Since the harvesting function h(t) varies with time due to the sine term there is no single constant equilibrium value P_e that remains fixed forever. However if we look for the average equilibrium or the condition where the population balances the average harvest we can solve for P. The average value of the sine function over a full cycle is zero so the average harvest is 10 turtles per year. Using r equals 0.1 and K equals 1000 we solve 0 equals 0.1P times one minus P over 1000 minus 10. This simplifies to 0 equals 0.1P minus 0.0001P squared minus 10. Rearranging gives 0.0001P squared minus 0.1P plus 10 equals zero. Multiplying by 10000 yields P squared minus 1000P plus 100000 equals zero. Using the quadratic formula we find P equals 1000 plus or minus the square root of 1000000 minus 400000 all divided by 2. This gives P equals 1000 plus or minus the square root of 600000 divided by 2. The square root of 600000 is approximately 774.6. So the two possible equilibrium points are approximately 887.3 and 112.7 turtles. The lower value is the unstable equilibrium and the higher value is the stable equilibrium under constant average conditions.

Now we analyze the case where the carrying capacity decreases linearly as K(t) equals 1000 minus 3t. The equilibrium condition at any instant t requires the growth rate to match the harvest rate. Assuming the harvest average remains 10 we set 0.1P times one minus P over (1000 minus 3t) equal to 10. This creates a time dependent equilibrium P_e(t). For the population to be sustainable we must have positive real solutions for P. The discriminant of the quadratic equation for P must be non negative. The equation is 0.1P minus 0.1P squared over K(t)
